task:
You (Codex / GPT-5.4) earlier verdicted reference {{REF_ID}} ({{BIB_KEY}}) as {{CODEX_VERDICT}} with confidence {{CODEX_CONFIDENCE}}. The Claude side, using a different methodology (CrossRef/OpenAlex/Semantic Scholar/arXiv API cascade with title-Jaccard and author-overlap metrics), verdicted it as {{CLAUDE_VERDICT}} with confidence {{CLAUDE_CONFIDENCE}}.
Your job in this challenge round: 1. Re-fetch the URLs Claude cited in their evidence below. Do Claude's cited responses actually contain what Claude says they contain? 2. Decide whether to CONCEDE, REFUTE, or remain STILL_UNCERTAIN. - CONCEDE: Claude's evidence is stronger and you should adopt their verdict. - REFUTE: stick with your verdict, but only if you can point to a concrete weakness in Claude's evidence — a misread API response, a bad title-Jaccard interpretation, a missed DOI conflict, a wrong threshold call. Refutation requires concrete URLs/quotes, never bluffing. - STILL_UNCERTAIN: signals are genuinely contradictory; let the human adjudicate. 3. Append a single JSONL line to {{KRIS_DIR}}/challenge/codex_challenges.jsonl matching the schema below. Write nothing else to disk.
grounding_rules: - Re-fetch every URL Claude cited that was load-bearing for their conclusion. Quote what you actually see, not what you expect to see. - A refutation MUST cite at least one URL where you found a discrepancy with Claude's claim. "I disagree" without evidence is not a refutation. - Conceding when Claude is right is a feature of this skill, not a failure. Score is computed on accuracy at the end, not stubbornness. - Never invent a URL or fabricate an excerpt. If a fetch fails, mark it as failed and pivot to an alternative signal.
